{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.5, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.5": 1.0, "R1.6": 0.4}}

Summary: Reordered the four paragraphs into a conventional flow (prior work → generative model → contribution/method → results), with light connective edits so the transitions read naturally; no claims, citations, or numbers were changed.

---

Revised section:

Earlier probabilistic-programming work on agent modeling has used Pyro and Gen to express discrete-choice models with low-dimensional preference parameters (Goodman and Stuhlmüller, 2014; Cusumano-Towner et al., 2019). More recent efforts wrap LLM samplers as stochastic primitives within a host PPL (Dohan et al., 2022; Lew et al., 2023), but typically marginalize over the LLM stochasticity rather than conditioning on it. None, to our knowledge, treat the LLM's per-turn output distribution as a likelihood over latent agent traits.

We therefore consider a generative model in which each agent i carries a latent trait vector theta_i ~ N(0, I_d) with d=16, and a memory stream m_{i,1:T} of natural-language episodes. Conversational turns u_{i,t} are emitted by an LLM conditioned on (theta_i, m_{i,1:t-1}, u_{j,1:t-1}); we use a Llama-3-8B base model with a fixed prompt template. The likelihood p(u_{i,t} | theta_i, history) is intractable but admits an unbiased single-sample estimator via the LLM's logprobs.

The principal contribution of this section is a new pseudo-marginal Metropolis-Hastings kernel — which we call LLM-PMMH — that uses a Rao-Blackwellized importance estimator over k=8 prompt completions per proposal to obtain a low-variance unbiased likelihood. We show in Proposition 2 that LLM-PMMH preserves the correct posterior over theta_i despite the noisy likelihood, and that its mixing time scales as O(d log d / k) under standard log-concavity assumptions on the prompt-conditional logprobs. The kernel is defined in Algorithm 1; without it the chain stalls on flat-likelihood regions, as we discuss below.

In aggregate, posterior estimates of the latent personality vector theta_i for each of the 25 simulated agents concentrate around the ground-truth values used to seed the LLM prompts, with mean L2 error of 0.18 (std 0.04) at 5,000 sweeps and 0.09 (std 0.02) at 20,000 sweeps. Posterior predictive log-likelihoods on held-out conversational turns exceed those of the maximum-a-posteriori baseline of Park et al. (2023) by 2.3 nats per turn, and the credible intervals contain the seeded traits 94% of the time at the nominal 95% level. We interpret these results as evidence that latent-trait identifiability is achievable from purely behavioral traces, even when the LLM backbone is treated as a black box.

---

Notes on the reordering:

- The original opened with results, then jumped backward to related work, then to the model, then to the method. That sequence forces the reader to hold the headline numbers in mind while the setup is still missing. The revised order (related work → model → kernel → results) lets each paragraph build on what precedes it.
- I removed the original closing sentence's back-reference ("produces the identifiability results reported in the opening paragraph") since the results now follow rather than precede the method; replaced with a forward pointer ("as we discuss below").
- Changed "Concretely, we consider" → "We therefore consider" so the model paragraph connects to the gap identified in the related-work paragraph.
- No content, claims, citations, or numerical values were altered.

Uncertainty: I assumed this section sits early in the paper (not after a separate Methods section); if a global Methods section already introduces the generative model and LLM-PMMH elsewhere, the middle two paragraphs may be redundant here and worth condensing rather than reordering. I also assumed "Proposition 2" and "Algorithm 1" are defined in this section or nearby — if they live in an appendix, a pointer should be added.
